preamble: The phonetic transformation from the alveolar nasal /n/ to the voiced alveolar fricative /z/ is a documented historical shift that involves both denasalization and fricativization. This process entails the loss of nasality characteristic of /n/ and the development of turbulent airflow, resulting in the fricative /z/. Such changes often occur in specific phonetic environments, frequently as part of broader lenition processes.

examples.note: In Pre-Paha, a medial *-n- denasalized and lenited to -z- before a presyllable that was subsequently lost.
An initial *CV-n- in Proto-Kra developed into z- in Qiaoshang.

sources: Matisoff, J. A. (2015). Amaravati: Abode of Amritas. University of California Press.
